skill.name: security-compliance-compliance-check
skill.description: Guides compliance readiness reviews for GDPR, HIPAA, SOC 2, PCI DSS, and related software controls.
skill.summary: Assess compliance gaps and plan practical controls for regulated software systems.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged command execution, network, environment access, C2 keywords, and weak cryptography patterns. Review found no prompt injection or confirmed malicious intent; most high keyword hits are compliance terminology, markdown fences, or illustrative code. The skill still carries medium risk because it includes executable CI commands, secret placeholders, and compliance code samples users may copy into production.

medium_findings.title: Executable Compliance Automation Examples
medium_findings.description: The playbook includes a GitHub Actions workflow that runs local compliance scripts and a license checker. This is a legitimate compliance automation pattern, but copied commands could execute unreviewed project code in CI.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The workflow plainly contains run steps for local scripts. The context is benign compliance automation, so the risk is misuse or unreviewed execution rather than malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: Secret Placeholder in Audit Hashing Example
medium_findings.description: The audit log integrity example concatenates SECRET_KEY into a hash calculation. This is not credential exfiltration, but copied code may encourage weak key management or ad hoc message authentication.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code sample clearly references SECRET_KEY in hashing logic. It is illustrative, but the security pattern should be reviewed before use.

medium_findings.title: Illustrative Compliance Code Requires Production Hardening
medium_findings.description: The playbook provides broad Python examples for consent, erasure, audit logging, HIPAA, and PCI DSS controls. These examples are guidance snippets, not complete validated implementations.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples are explicitly framed as implementation patterns. They touch regulated data handling, so incomplete adoption could create compliance or security gaps.

low_findings.title: Static High-Severity Keywords Are Mostly Compliance Terms
low_findings.description: C2 and weak-cryptography detections map to terms such as SOC2, PCI-DSS, SHA256, HSTS, and cardholder data. No evidence found that these keywords are used for command-and-control behavior or malicious cryptography.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Direct review showed the flagged words appear in regulatory framework names or defensive security examples. No prompt-injection or exfiltration intent was present.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backticks Triggered Command Heuristics
low_findings.description: Many external command alerts correspond to markdown code fences or resource references. These are documentation delimiters, not active code execution by the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious backtick pattern is explained by markdown formatting. The remaining executable commands are contained in an example CI workflow.

low_findings.title: Network Finding Is a Local Method Example
low_findings.description: The network alert maps to a right-to-erasure method that notifies processors. No evidence found of hardcoded endpoints, unauthorized network calls, or data exfiltration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed code is a compliance workflow example with a method call name, not an HTTP client call. The surrounding text concerns GDPR erasure handling.

dangerous_patterns.title: Copy-Paste Shell Execution in CI Example
dangerous_patterns.description: The workflow example includes shell run steps that execute Python scripts and a dependency license command. Users should review script sources and permissions before adding this to CI.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit and executable if copied into a workflow. They are not hidden or malicious in this skill, but they are a meaningful operational risk.

content.value_statement: Regulated teams need clear evidence of privacy, security, and audit controls. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code map gaps and plan practical remediation.
actual_capabilities: Maps product context to GDPR, HIPAA, SOC 2, and PCI DSS obligations.
Builds gap analyses with severity, evidence needs, and remediation steps.
Suggests privacy, access control, audit logging, and data retention controls.
Creates audit evidence checklists and compliance reporting outlines.
Provides example automation patterns for recurring compliance checks.
limitations: Does not provide legal advice or formal certification.
Requires accurate system scope, data flows, and control evidence.
Example code must be reviewed before production use.
Cannot verify compliance without an authorized audit process.

use_cases.title: Prepare a SaaS SOC 2 Review
use_cases.description: Map current controls to trust service criteria and create an evidence request list.

use_cases.title: Plan Privacy Controls
use_cases.description: Assess GDPR consent, erasure, portability, retention, and processor management requirements.

use_cases.title: Improve Payment Data Handling
use_cases.description: Review PCI DSS control needs for tokenization, storage limits, network segmentation, and monitoring.

prompt_templates.title: Identify Applicable Regulations
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this product description and data flow. Identify which regulations may apply and explain the evidence needed for each one.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Gap Analysis
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare our current controls against GDPR, HIPAA, SOC 2, and PCI DSS. Rank gaps by severity and implementation effort.

prompt_templates.title: Draft a Remediation Roadmap
prompt_templates.prompt: Build a 90-day compliance remediation plan with owners, milestones, evidence artifacts, and verification steps.

prompt_templates.title: Design Continuous Monitoring
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a continuous compliance monitoring workflow for our repository, cloud controls, audit logs, and evidence reporting.

output_examples.input: Assess whether our analytics product needs GDPR controls.
output_examples.output: A regulation applicability matrix, data processing questions, likely GDPR obligations, and a prioritized evidence checklist.

output_examples.input: Prepare SOC 2 evidence for access controls.
output_examples.output: A control-to-evidence checklist covering MFA, role reviews, access approvals, audit logs, and exception handling.

output_examples.input: Review our PCI DSS storage approach.
output_examples.output: A risk summary for stored payment data, tokenization recommendations, prohibited data checks, and monitoring tasks.

best_practices: Provide current policies, system boundaries, data categories, vendors, and audit evidence.
Treat generated controls as draft guidance for legal, security, and compliance review.
Test automation examples in a controlled environment before CI or production use.
anti_patterns: Do not claim certification based only on generated output.
Do not paste secrets, PHI, payment data, or personal data into prompts.
Do not deploy sample code without threat modeling, testing, and owner approval.

faq.question: Can this skill certify my product as compliant?
faq.answer: No. It supports readiness work, but formal certification requires authorized auditors and legal review.

faq.question: Which frameworks does it cover?
faq.answer: It focuses on GDPR, HIPAA, SOC 2, PCI DSS, and common supporting security controls.

faq.question: Can it generate policies?
faq.answer: It can draft policy outlines and evidence lists. Counsel and compliance owners should approve final documents.

faq.question: Does it run scans automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and example automation patterns that users must review and implement.

faq.question: Is it safe for sensitive data?
faq.answer: Use summaries and redacted examples. Do not include secrets, PHI, card data, or personal records.

faq.question: Can Claude Code or Codex use it?
faq.answer: Yes. The metadata supports Claude, Codex, and Claude Code workflows.
